sections.title: On Our Way Back Home
sections.content_summary: Navya and her grandfather walk home from school via the market route, noticing neighbourhood services along the way. They pass a postbox and discuss how the postal system works — letters are collected by postmen, sorted at the post office by region, and dispatched to their destinations. The section traces the evolution of communication from ancient drumbeats and rock carvings, to messengers and pigeons, to postal services, telephone, radio, television, and finally mobile phones and the internet. The contrast between the slow pace of handwritten letters and the instant speed of digital communication is explored through a personal intergenerational conversation.

tasks.task_text: Write a letter to a friend and post it.
Teacher can take students on a field trip to a post office, to explore various activities that take place there.
Have you or your family members ever sent, or received a letter or a parcel?
How does the post office help people stay connected with each other?

Discuss with the elders in your neighbourhood and find out how they communicated with each other when they were of your age. Compare their answers to how you and your friends communicate today; and fill in the table below:
Letters took a long time to reach other persons. How do you think it affected communication between people in the past?
How is it different from the way people communicate now?
Today quick messages make sharing the news easier, but sometimes wrong information spreads very quickly too. How can we ensure the news we share is true?

sections.title: Savings for the Future
sections.content_summary: Navya and her grandfather pass a bank with an ATM on their way home and discuss the purpose of banks — keeping money safe and saving for future needs. The section contrasts the past (long bank queues, money orders through the post office) with the present (instant mobile-phone transfers, UPI). The text introduces the ATM (Automated Teller Machine) as a technology that allows round-the-clock cash access. The chapter also introduces the bank passbook as a document recording account details, connecting banking to civic literacy.

tasks.task_text: If you had to send money to someone, which method would you choose—using a phone, Money Order or some other mode? Why?
If you get pocket money, do you save it? If yes, where do you put your savings?
Why do you think it is important to save money?
What are some things you could do to save more money?
Request one of your family members to show you their bank passbook. Note the important elements given on the first page of the passbook.

Visit a bank in your locality or find out from your elders about how it works. After that, answer the following questions: (a) Other than saving money, what else do people use banks for? (b) What is an ATM and how does it help people?

Take an Indian currency note. Look at it closely and answer the following questions: (a) What is the value of the note? (b) How many languages do you see on the note? (c) What pictures or symbols are there on the note?

sections.title: Looking Around the Neighbourhood
sections.content_summary: The section introduces the concept of public places — hospitals, banks, post offices, police stations, libraries, bus stations — as community infrastructure that serves health, safety, cleanliness, and the environment. Navya draws a map of her neighbourhood, learning cardinal directions (N, S, E, W) and how they are conventionally represented on paper. The section also includes a 'Do you know?' box on the barter system, explaining how the difficulty of finding matching trade partners led to the invention of money. Transportation infrastructure — metro trains, flyovers — is discussed alongside their environmental trade-off (loss of trees, birds, animals), with Dada ji suggesting balance through tree-planting. The hospital sub-episode introduces community health workers (doctors, nurses) and the concept of health camps.

tasks.task_text: Which mode of transportation do you use the most?
Can you identify the structures in the pictures, and explain how they have made travel faster and connectivity better?
How did your parents and grandparents go to school or other places, when they were of your age? Is it different from how you travel today? Note it down below.
List the people who works at a hospital or a health centre; and write down their role in the following table. One example has been given in the table.
Look at the picture above, and write down the places located in the North, East, West, and South of Navya's home.
Look at the picture on the next page, and imagine your school at the centre. Name the buildings or areas near your school and write the direction they are situated in the given spaces.
Imagine you are giving directions to a friend to visit your favourite place near your school. How would you explain it using directions (left, right, forward, backward, etc.) and landmarks?
Draw a map showing the route from your school to the public library or any such place near your school. Identify landmarks like parks, shops, hospitals, bus stops, etc. on the map.

Imagine you have ₹100 to utilise in a month. Your task is to plan how to spend and save wisely. Think about your expenses for stationery, snacks, toys, etc. Decide how much you will save in your piggy bank and how much you will use for your expenses. After making this plan, think about how saving money could help you in the future. Use the table below to create your budget:

Imagine that your group is in charge of making your neighbourhood better. Discuss what new services or facilities you would add or improve. Present how these changes would help everyone in the community.

As a class, take help of the elders around you to list people in your neighbourhood who helped the community during COVID-19. These could include doctors, nurses, garbage collectors, shopkeepers, teachers and others. Discuss how they made the community safer and why it is important to thank them.
Can you list out the people involved and vehicles used in each of these services in the boxes given below? An example has been given.

dual_strand.natural: Human-built infrastructure — transportation networks, communication systems, and urban public services
dual_strand.human_cultural: Civic responsibility, community interdependence, and the evolution of communication and financial practices across generations
